title: Project cloning ("send project to")
description: On invite acceptance, create an independent copy of the source project owned by the recipient. Preferred path (EPIC-100 landed): items are deep-copied, and their tracked catalogue products are referenced via new item_products rows pointing at the same shared global products — no product row is duplicated. Fallback path (EPIC-100 not yet landed): items and their item-scoped products are deep-copied, matching today's schema. Listings, listing_matches, price observations, and auction snapshots are never copied in either path.
acceptance_criteria: Accepting an invite creates a new projects row owned by the recipient (owner_user_id = recipient), independent of the source project

Preferred path: items are deep-copied; for each source item_products row, a new item_products row is created for the recipient's cloned item pointing at the SAME global products.id (reference, not copy) — verified by asserting the cloned item's product row id equals the source's
Fallback path (only if EPIC-100 has not landed): items and their item-scoped products (terms, match_terms, prices, notes) are deep-copied into the new project/items, matching today's schema

Listings, listing_matches, product_price_observations, product_new_price_history, and auction_snapshots are explicitly NOT copied in either path — the clone starts with none and accumulates its own via its own watch cycle

Mutating the clone after creation never affects the source project, and vice versa (no shared foreign keys back to the original beyond an optional provenance pointer for display purposes); in the preferred path, both projects may legitimately point at the same shared product row without that counting as a violation of independence
Cloning a project the inviter no longer owns (e.g. deleted or transferred) fails cleanly rather than partially copying
notes: See docs/adr/0006-project-sharing-invites-and-cloning.md and docs/adr/0007-catalogue-globalization.md. EPIC-100 is a hard blocking dependency for this epic per ADR-0001 — the fallback path exists only to document what happens if that sequencing is not honoured, not as an equally-acceptable alternative.
